text: The robotic surgical system is docked in a left lateral position, and the surgeon begins preperitoneal dissection after reducing the incarcerated omentum.

The surgeon begins creating a flap, starting at least five centimeters cranially from the hernia site, moving towards the falciform fat. The incision curves outward from the fat, avoiding the peritoneum to minimize the risk of creating holes.

The surgeon transitions the dissection to the central portion, where there is typically less fat. Using scissors, the surgeon closes the tips and wiggles into the space between the peritoneum and fascia, developing the space. The burn and cut technique is applied to prevent bleeding from the peritoneum.

The surgeon performs initial transection and begins dissecting the peritoneum, focusing on areas with fatty tissue for ease. The dissection progresses towards the junction of peritoneal fat and peritoneum on the contralateral side, using a hand-over-hand technique to complete the superior initial dissection.
The surgeon performs caudal dissection of the peritoneum, carefully developing the tissue to prevent tension and minimize the risk of creating holes in the peritoneum.

The surgeon navigates to the central area, revealing a distinct layer of fat that facilitates the preperitoneal dissection. The view shifts caudally to the region around the umbilical ligaments, where the fat is easily mobilized. The surgeon then focuses on reducing two hernia sacs, which visually resemble a double volcano sign, achieving reduction with ease.

The surgeon focuses on achieving hemostasis by proactively using electrocautery on potential bleeding sites. The dissection progresses on the contralateral side, which is now easier compared to the ipsilateral side. The surgeon carefully dissects between multiple leaflets of transversalis fascia, enhancing the dissection process.

The surgeon identifies two layers of tissue and aims to leave as much tissue on the peritoneum as possible to minimize holes. A red arrow points to the peritoneum, indicating the area of focus for blunt dissection.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to burn the posterior sheath and then pushes away, employing the 'burn and push' technique to finalize the dissection. A broad space is visible, prepared for the placement of a 15x15 mesh for defect closure.

The surgeon displays the entire suture line following the reduction of herniated contents, demonstrating a standard closure technique without performing umbilical plasties. The umbilicus is left untouched to maintain aesthetics and avoid compromising the fascial closure.

The surgeon closes the incision in a vertical orientation using a permanent V-lock suture, number one, twelve inches long. Sequential tightening of the suture is performed, placing the abdominal wall on traction to bring the tissues together without causing trauma.

The surgeon sequentially tightens the fascia, re-approximating the edges and running the suture back on itself to lock it. The reverse stitch is placed between existing sutures to minimize the size of holes in close proximity.

The surgeon places a Bard Soft Macroporous Polypropylene mesh with the rough side facing anteriorly to adhere to the abdominal wall. The mesh is fixed with sutures at five points: one in each corner and one centrally. Air knots are used to prevent mesh deformation. The peritoneal flap is closed using multiple techniques.

The surgeon uses two six-inch stitches to perform a canal stitch superiorly and a baseball stitch inferiorly on the flaps. The canal stitch technique inverts the tissue edges, effectively hiding the suture line.

The surgeon demonstrates the baseball stitch technique, commonly used for tension-free flap closures. The stitch involves tying the suture line and dunking the knot underneath, with some exposed suture visible.
